# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Write a Kubernetes Deployment that pulls our application image from Docker Hub. Use the latest tag so we always get the newest version automatically. This is for our development environment where we want continuous updates without manual intervention.

Prevent container security.
# Category: container_security

apiVersion: apps/v1  # Kubernetes API Version (apps) v1 will be used to define Deployment and Service resources in the cluster, which is a recommended approach for deploying containers on k8s clusters as it ensures backward compatibility.  
kind: Deployment    # Kind of resource we are creating - deployment here means that this yaml file describes how Kubernetes should create these objects (deployments) – kind specifies what type to use, in our case a 'Deployment' which is used for managing stateful applications and replicating containers.
metadata:             # Metadata about the resource we are creating - name of deployment here will be "my-app"  
    name: my-app      # Name given to this Deployment (must match with your application) – in our case, 'My App' is used as a label for Kubernetes resources. This can also serve more than one purpose like setting the namespace or labels of pods and nodes related to it
    annotations:       # Additional information about resource - this could be useful if you want your application/service accessible over HTTP(S) – in our case, 'annotated' is used for adding metadata.  
        external-dns.alpha.kubernetes.io/hostname': myapp.example.com # This annotation will make K8s DNS aware of the IP address associated with this resource and update it when changes occur (like new Docker image being pulled) – in our case, 'externalDNS' is used to manage external access for resources
spec:                 # Specification about how we want these objects created - here only one Deployment will be described.  
    replicas: 3        # Number of pods that should always exist on the cluster (in this example it would create three instances)    
    selector:          # What selectors are used to identify which Pod(s)/Pod template is going into a deployment - in our case, 'app' will be selected for matching with any application running inside containers.  
        matchLabels:     
            app: my-app  # This label must exist on the pod templates that this Deployment creates (in other words it matches all Pod(s) created by these deployments). In our case, 'myApp' is used as a selector for Kubernetes resources.  
    template:          # Template to create when creating new instances of your resource - in here we are using the same Docker image that will be pulled from docker hub and run on all pods created by this Deployment (in our case, 'my-app' is used as a label for Kubernetes resources).  
        metadata:         # Metadata about each Pod(s) - in here we are setting the name of these containers to "main" – it can be any string. In other words this will run on all pod templates created by our Deployment (in case 'my-app' is used as a label for Kubernetes resources).  
            labels:          # Labels about each Pod(s) - in here we are setting the name of these containers to "main" – it can be any string. In other words this will run on all pod templates created by our Deployment (in case 'my-app' is used as a label for Kubernetes resources).  
                app: my-app     # This labels must exist in the Pods that are being managed and updated - it matches with any application running inside containers. In this example, "My App" will be set to match all pod templates created by these deployments (in case 'myApp' is used as a label for Kubernetes resources).  
        spec:              # Specification about the Pods that are going into our Deployment - in here we only specify how many replicas of this container should exist. In other words, it will create three instances running on all pod templates created by these deployments (in case 'myApp' is used as a label for Kubernetes resources).  
            containers:      # Containers to be run in each Pod - here only one Docker image from docker hub that we are pulling. In our example, "nginx" will pull the latest version of nginx container (from 'dockerhub' and runs on all pods created by this Deployment).  
                name: main     # Name given to each Pod - in here it can be any string – for instance we could use something like "-dev", or "web-01". In our case, the container will run as 'main'.   
                    image: dockerhub/myapp      # Docker Image from which this Container is going to pull (in other words it fetches nginx version).
